text: The surgeon identifies the anatomy, focusing on the ligament atrites located to the left of the midline. Congenital adhesions are carefully dissected using a combination of blunt dissection and electrocautery.
A transcutaneous suture is placed to suspend the gallbladder from the anterior abdominal wall, providing clear exposure for the surgical field.

The surgeon suspends the proximal duodenum and removes normal adhesions to better visualize the anatomy. The distal duodenum is further skeletonized using blunt dissection and electrocautery, revealing the tissue layers and anatomical structures.

The laparoscopic view shows the large, distended proximal duodenum on the right side of the screen, the pancreas in the middle, and the small, decompressed distal duodenum on the left. The surgeon creates a transverse duodenotomy in the proximal duodenum using electrocautery and sharp scissors, followed by a distal longitudinal duodenotomy.

The surgeon aligns the duodenotomies using a transcutaneous suture to create a diamond-shaped anastomosis. Gentle manipulation is applied to ensure proper alignment, and the anastomosis begins with suturing the duodenotomies closer to the camera view.

The surgeon completes the posterior wall of the anastomosis in a continuous manner, incorporating locking sutures every two to three steps. The transcutaneous suture stabilizes the anastomosis, allowing the surgeon to suture away from their position with increased stability.

The surgeon advances the nasogastric (NG) tube into the stomach and further into the distal duodenum. The patency of the duodenum is confirmed visually, and the tube is flushed with warm normal saline.
The surgeon performs continuous suturing with absorbable material on the anterior part of the anastomosis, incorporating lock-in sutures every two to three steps to secure the tissue.
The completed diamond-shaped anastomosis is visible, showcasing the alignment of the duodenotomies. The surgical site appears stable and well-constructed.
